narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like coming from an upper-middle-class family and planning to attend full-time like most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 60.5% chance that you would fail it—above the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This decision was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, especially your lower decile ranking in Year 3 grades compared to typical students, which had the strongest pull toward predicting failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in a lower decile than average, reinforced this concern as the next biggest factor pushing the prediction in that direction. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out positively, as it was strong enough to counter some of that risk and nudge the model toward expecting success. Your undergraduate GPA and family income level also helped a bit, showing you're above average in those areas relative to others in the dataset. Being full-time, like over 90% of students, provided a small favorable influence too. Overall, while your test scores and background offered some reassurance, your school deciles were the standout issues when compared to peers who typically pass the bar, tipping the scales against admission. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many factors beyond these metrics shape success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive impact or strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program. You might also seek feedback from pre-law advisors familiar with profiles like yours, and reapply after addressing these key areas. Your journey as a Black woman in law is inspiring, and with targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor next time. We're rooting for you.
